title: Concrete slab cost: 10x10, 12x12, 20x20, 30x30
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed slab, pad, patio, and foundation prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. A poured concrete slab in 2026 runs $7 to $13 per square foot installed for a standard 4-inch residential pad, with the national typical near $9.25 per square foot. That figure is normalized for 3,500 PSI ready-mix delivery, 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh, control joints, and a single mobilization fee. Small slabs carry the highest per-square-foot rate because the minimum-yard surcharge from the ready-mix supplier is absorbed across less area — a 10x10 pad lands closer to $9-$18 per square foot, while a 30x30 slab drops to $5.50-$13. The largest variable across every slab size is what sits on top of it: a shed pad and a garage floor are spec'd differently, and the price moves with the spec.

faqs.question: How much does a 10x10 concrete slab cost?
faqs.answer: A 10x10 concrete slab (100 square feet) costs $700 to $1,800 installed in 2026, with a national typical near $1,100. The per-square-foot rate is high because the ready-mix minimum-yard surcharge is absorbed across only 100 square feet. Shed pads, AC condenser pads, and small storage building bases land in this size range.

faqs.question: How much does a 12x12 concrete slab cost?
faqs.answer: A 12x12 concrete slab (144 square feet) costs $900 to $2,100 installed in 2026, with a national typical near $1,400. This size is common for hot tub pads, small patios, and gazebo bases. The pour itself takes 1.5 cubic yards of concrete for a 4-inch slab — most ready-mix suppliers carry a 3-yard minimum, so smaller pours pay for unused yardage.

faqs.question: How much does a 20x20 concrete slab cost?
faqs.answer: A 20x20 concrete slab (400 square feet) costs $2,400 to $5,200 installed in 2026, with a national typical near $3,600. This is a common size for one-car garages, large sheds, and outdoor kitchens. At 400 square feet the per-square-foot rate drops because mobilization and minimum-yard costs are spread across more area.

faqs.question: How much does a 30x30 concrete slab cost?
faqs.answer: A 30x30 concrete slab (900 square feet) costs $4,800 to $11,700 installed in 2026, with a national typical near $7,800. This size fits a two-car garage floor, workshop, or detached accessory dwelling foundation. At this footprint the per-square-foot rate hits its lowest point because mobilization is fully amortized.

faqs.question: What is the cost per cubic yard of concrete in 2026?
faqs.answer: Ready-mix concrete delivered to residential sites runs $145-$200 per cubic yard in 2026, depending on PSI rating and regional supplier pricing. Most residential pours also include a short-load surcharge of $50-$120 if the order is under the supplier's minimum (typically 3-5 cubic yards). According to the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, average national wholesale ready-mix pricing was up roughly 8 percent year-over-year heading into 2026.

faqs.question: How thick should a concrete slab be?
faqs.answer: Slab thickness depends on the load. Sheds and hot tub pads use 4 inches. Single-car garage floors and workshops use 4-5 inches with rebar. Two-car garages and RV pads use 5-6 inches with rebar. Each additional inch of thickness adds about $0.75-$1.25 per square foot. Going thinner than 4 inches on any vehicle-traffic slab is a structural shortcut that shows up within 5 years.

faqs.question: Does a concrete slab need rebar?
faqs.answer: Reinforcement is required on any slab that will hold vehicle weight or any slab over 100 square feet. Standard residential practice is #4 rebar on 18-inch centers for vehicle-traffic slabs, and 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh for foot-traffic slabs. According to the American Concrete Institute, fiber-mesh additive does not replace steel for structural load resistance — it only controls plastic shrinkage cracking during curing.

faqs.question: How long does it take to pour a concrete slab?
faqs.answer: A residential concrete slab takes 1-2 days to pour and finish, plus 7 days of curing before foot traffic and 28 days before full design strength. Day 1 is layout, excavation, base, forms, and reinforcement. Day 2 is pour, finish, and joint cuts. Heavy loads — vehicles, RVs, equipment — should wait the full 28 days before going on the slab.

howToSteps.name: Lay out and stake the slab
howToSteps.text: The contractor stakes the slab corners, confirms square with the 3-4-5 method, and verifies grade. Vehicle-traffic slabs need 1-2 percent slope away from any structure.

howToSteps.name: Excavate and compact the subgrade
howToSteps.text: Topsoil and organics are stripped to 6-8 inches below final grade. The exposed soil is compacted with a plate compactor or jumping jack. Soft spots are dug out and replaced with compacted gravel.

howToSteps.name: Install gravel base and forms
howToSteps.text: A 4-inch compacted layer of crushed stone is installed. Lumber forms are staked along the perimeter to set the slab elevation. On vehicle-traffic slabs a vapor barrier is laid over the gravel before pouring.

howToSteps.name: Place reinforcement
howToSteps.text: On vehicle-traffic slabs, #4 rebar on 18-inch centers is tied in a grid at mid-slab depth. On foot-traffic slabs, 6-by-6 W2.9 wire mesh is set on chairs. Bar laps a minimum of 12 inches.

howToSteps.name: Pour and screed
howToSteps.text: 3,500 PSI ready-mix is placed via truck chute, pump, or wheelbarrow. The crew screeds with a straight edge, then bull-floats to bring the cream to the surface.

howToSteps.name: Cut control joints
howToSteps.text: Control joints are cut at 24-30 times slab thickness (10 feet on a 4-inch slab) with a walk-behind saw, typically 6-12 hours after pour. Joint depth is 1/4 of slab thickness.

howToSteps.name: Cure the slab
howToSteps.text: A curing compound is sprayed on the surface or the slab is kept damp under plastic for 7 days. Foot traffic at 24 hours, vehicle traffic at 7-10 days, heavy loads at 28 days.

content:
Quick Answer: A residential concrete slab costs $7 to $13 per square foot installed in 2026. By size: 10x10 runs $700-$1,800, 12x12 runs $900-$2,100, 20x20 runs $2,400-$5,200, and 30x30 runs $4,800-$11,700. The per-square-foot rate drops as the slab gets bigger because ready-mix minimum-yard surcharges and mobilization are absorbed across more area.

Concrete slab pricing is one of the most asked-about line items in residential concrete, and one of the most miscalculated by homeowners using per-square-foot averages without accounting for the size penalty on small pours. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting, so the dollar figures on every page reflect what the slab actually cost to put in the ground. This guide breaks 2026 slab pricing down by the four most common residential sizes: 10x10, 12x12, 20x20, and 30x30.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed slab, pad, patio, and foundation prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. A poured concrete slab in 2026 runs $7 to $13 per square foot installed for a standard 4-inch residential pad, with the national typical near $9.25 per square foot. That figure is normalized for 3,500 PSI ready-mix delivery, 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh, control joints, and a single mobilization fee. Small slabs carry the highest per-square-foot rate because the minimum-yard surcharge from the ready-mix supplier is absorbed across less area — a 10x10 pad lands closer to $9-$18 per square foot, while a 30x30 slab drops to $5.50-$13. The largest variable across every slab size is what sits on top of it: a shed pad and a garage floor are spec'd differently, and the price moves with the spec.

Slab cost by size: 2026 national ranges

The table below shows 2026 national pricing for the four most common residential slab sizes, in standard 4-inch broom finish with 6-by-6 wire mesh. Vehicle-traffic slabs (garages, RV pads) bump up an additional 15-25 percent for thicker concrete and rebar reinforcement.

SizeSquare feetCubic yards (4")LowNational typicalHigh
10' x 10'100 sq ft1.25 cy$700$1,100$1,800
12' x 12'144 sq ft1.8 cy$900$1,400$2,100
20' x 20'400 sq ft5 cy$2,400$3,600$5,200
30' x 30'900 sq ft11.25 cy$4,800$7,800$11,700

Per-square-foot rate by size: a 10x10 lands at $7-$18, a 12x12 at $6.25-$15, a 20x20 at $6-$13, and a 30x30 at $5.30-$13. The per-square-foot drop with size is real — it is not a rounding artifact. Mobilization, minimum-yard surcharges, and crew setup are largely fixed costs, so spreading them across 900 square feet vs 100 square feet drops the unit rate by 30-50 percent.

Why small slabs cost more per square foot

A 10x10 pad uses 1.25 cubic yards of concrete at 4 inches thick. The local ready-mix supplier typically charges a short-load surcharge for orders under 3-5 cubic yards — somewhere between $50 and $120. That surcharge alone adds $0.50-$1.20 per square foot on a 10x10. Mobilization (truck, crew, equipment) is another fixed cost that does not scale down — paying a crew for half a day costs the same whether they pour 100 square feet or 200 square feet.

According to the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, average national wholesale ready-mix pricing was up roughly 8 percent heading into 2026, and short-load surcharges have followed. For a homeowner debating slab size, batching two small pads into a single visit (one shed pad + one AC pad on the same day) is meaningfully cheaper than two separate visits.

10x10 slab deep dive

The 10x10 slab is the most common small residential pour. It fits a 10x10 storage shed, a 10x10 gazebo, a hot tub pad, or a generator base. Typical 2026 install: $1,100 for a 4-inch broom-finish slab with wire mesh. Real-world range is $700-$1,800 driven by:

  • Access: direct truck chute = baseline. Wheelbarrow = +$150-$300.
  • Base prep: flat well-drained ground = baseline. Slope correction or soft soil = +$200-$400.
  • Reinforcement: wire mesh = baseline. #4 rebar = +$60-$120.
  • Finish: broom = baseline. Integral color = +$100-$200. Stamped = +$400-$700.

12x12 slab deep dive

A 12x12 slab covers a small patio, hot tub deck, or 12x12 storage building. It hits 144 square feet and 1.8 cubic yards at 4 inches. Typical 2026 install: $1,400. The 12x12 is the smallest size where adding 1 extra inch of thickness for additional load (going to 5 inches) is consistently worth the $100-$170 upcharge — it doubles the load capacity. Hot tub manufacturers usually require a minimum 4-inch slab; many spec 5 inches for full-water hot tubs over 6,000 pounds. The Cost of Concrete concrete patios hub has detailed pricing for upgraded finishes on this footprint.

20x20 slab deep dive

The 20x20 is the most common medium-size residential pour and the size where per-square-foot pricing starts to feel reasonable to homeowners. Typical uses: one-car garage floor, large workshop slab, outdoor kitchen pad, large covered-patio base. Typical 2026 install: $3,600 for a 4-inch broom finish. For garage and workshop use, the spec moves to 5 inches with #4 rebar on 18-inch centers — that bumps the price to $4,200-$5,800.

According to the International Code Council residential building code, any slab supporting a structure with a finished interior (garage, workshop, accessory dwelling) requires engineered footings tied to the slab perimeter. That can add $1,200-$3,000 to a 20x20 garage slab depending on frost depth and soil bearing capacity.

30x30 slab deep dive

The 30x30 is the standard two-car garage floor and the largest common residential single-pour slab. At 900 square feet and 11.25 cubic yards (4 inches) or 14 cubic yards (5 inches), it is also the smallest pour where most ready-mix suppliers will deliver without a short-load surcharge. Typical 2026 install: $7,800 for a 5-inch slab with #4 rebar on 18-inch centers.

At this footprint, the line items that move the price the most are not size-related — they are spec-related:

  • Slab thickness: 4-inch = $6,500-$8,500. 5-inch = $7,800-$10,200. 6-inch = $9,200-$11,700.
  • Reinforcement: wire mesh only = baseline. #4 rebar grid = +$0.60-$0.80 per square foot ($540-$720).
  • Vapor barrier: required on any enclosed-structure slab. +$0.30-$0.50 per square foot.
  • Insulation under slab: not required by code in most regions, but adds $1.50-$2.50 per square foot if specified.
  • Engineered footings: required for structure-supporting slabs. +$1,500-$4,000.

How a concrete slab gets poured

The pour itself is one day. The full project is 2-4 days. The steps below apply to any residential slab from 10x10 to 30x30 — the differences are crew size and pour duration, not method.

  1. Lay out and stake the slab. The contractor stakes the slab corners, confirms square with the 3-4-5 method, and verifies grade. Vehicle-traffic slabs need 1-2 percent slope away from any structure.
  2. Excavate and compact the subgrade. Topsoil and organics are stripped to 6-8 inches below final grade. The exposed soil is compacted with a plate compactor or jumping jack. Soft spots are dug out and replaced with compacted gravel.
  3. Install gravel base and forms. A 4-inch compacted layer of crushed stone is installed. Lumber forms are staked along the perimeter to set the slab elevation. On vehicle-traffic slabs a vapor barrier is laid over the gravel before pouring.
  4. Place reinforcement. On vehicle-traffic slabs, #4 rebar on 18-inch centers is tied in a grid at mid-slab depth. On foot-traffic slabs, 6-by-6 W2.9 wire mesh is set on chairs. Bar laps a minimum of 12 inches.
  5. Pour and screed. 3,500 PSI ready-mix is placed via truck chute, pump, or wheelbarrow. The crew screeds with a straight edge, then bull-floats to bring the cream to the surface.
  6. Cut control joints. Control joints are cut at 24-30 times slab thickness (10 feet on a 4-inch slab) with a walk-behind saw, typically 6-12 hours after pour. Joint depth is 1/4 of slab thickness.
  7. Cure the slab. A curing compound is sprayed on the surface or the slab is kept damp under plastic for 7 days. Foot traffic at 24 hours, vehicle traffic at 7-10 days, heavy loads at 28 days.

Reading a concrete slab quote

A complete slab quote names: square footage, slab thickness, PSI of the ready-mix, reinforcement type, joint spacing, finish, and any access notes. Quotes that compress those into a single lump are not necessarily dishonest but make line-by-line comparison impossible.

Common gaps in slab quotes: no PSI listed (assume 3,000 — under-spec for any freeze-thaw region), no rebar listed (assume mesh only — under-spec for any vehicle slab), no vapor barrier listed (will show as moisture problems in 1-3 years on enclosed slabs), and no short-load surcharge mentioned (gets added as a change-order line item later). According to the American Concrete Institute, the minimum recommended residential slab spec is 3,500 PSI ready-mix with control joints at 24-30 times slab thickness.

Regional variation

Slab pricing varies 25-40 percent between U.S. metros. Cheapest installed pricing in 2026 sits in the Midwest and lower South — Indianapolis, Kansas City, Memphis, Birmingham — where ready-mix is locally produced and labor markets are softer. Most expensive markets are Northeast and West Coast metros, where freeze-thaw mix surcharges and high labor rates push installed prices 30-50 percent above national typical. Homeowners can compare local installed slab pricing on the national best concrete contractors directory, or jump to a city listicle like Mooresville, NC for regional examples.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a 10x10 concrete slab cost?

A 10x10 concrete slab (100 square feet) costs $700 to $1,800 installed in 2026, with a national typical near $1,100. The per-square-foot rate is high because the ready-mix minimum-yard surcharge is absorbed across only 100 square feet. Shed pads, AC condenser pads, and small storage building bases land in this size range.

The pour itself takes 1.5 cubic yards of concrete for a 4-inch slab — most ready-mix suppliers carry a 3-yard minimum, so smaller pours pay for unused yardage.

At 400 square feet the per-square-foot rate drops because mobilization and minimum-yard costs are spread across more area.

This size fits a two-car garage floor, workshop, or detached accessory dwelling foundation. At this footprint the per-square-foot rate hits its lowest point because mobilization is fully amortized.

Most residential pours also include a short-load surcharge of $50-$120 if the order is under the supplier's minimum (typically 3-5 cubic yards). According to the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, average national wholesale ready-mix pricing was up roughly 8 percent year-over-year heading into 2026.

Slab thickness depends on the load. Sheds and hot tub pads use 4 inches. Single-car garage floors and workshops use 4-5 inches with rebar. Two-car garages and RV pads use 5-6 inches with rebar. Key takeaways

  • National typical 2026 slab pricing: 10x10 = $1,100, 12x12 = $1,400, 20x20 = $3,600, 30x30 = $7,800.
  • Per-square-foot rate drops sharply as the slab gets bigger — from $11/sq ft on a 10x10 to $8.65/sq ft on a 30x30 — because mobilization and minimum-yard surcharges are fixed costs.
  • Ready-mix delivered runs $145-$200 per cubic yard; short-load surcharges add $50-$120 on pours under 3-5 cubic yards.
  • 4-inch is the minimum residential slab thickness; vehicle-traffic and structure-supporting slabs need 5-6 inches with rebar.
  • Vapor barriers, engineered footings, and underslab insulation are spec-driven line items that can add $1,500-$5,000 to a garage-floor slab.
  • Batching small pours (multiple pads on the same visit) cuts the per-pad cost by avoiding repeat mobilization fees.

Need a contractor whose slab quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or jump to your city's page to compare local slab pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
